#  Typical Relaxor AFE systems and characteristics:

Typical relaxor AFE systems include KNN-based, NBT-based, AN-based, and other compositions, each exhibiting distinct structural and electrical characteristics. KNN-based systems often display diffuse phase transitions and slim P-E loops due to local compositional heterogeneity, while NBT-based relaxors show strong frequency dispersion and high polarization but suffer from large hysteresis. AN-based systems, such as AgNbO₃, demonstrate antiferroelectricity with moderate energy storage but limited thermal stability. A comprehensive summary of these systems, including their compositions, crystal structures, dielectric properties, and energy storage metrics (W_rec, η, breakdown strength), is provided in Table 1.

Despite extensive studies, several critical aspects remain unclear. First, the precise structural origin of the relaxor behavior in these AFE systems is often attributed to polar nanoregions (PNRs), yet the relationship between PNR dynamics and hysteresis reduction is not quantitatively established. Second, the trade-off between W_rec and η is empirically observed but lacks a mechanistic framework linking local dipolar heterogeneity to macroscopic performance. Third, the role of chemical disorder in modulating the AFE-to-FE transition field (E_AF/E_FA) is poorly understood, with most studies relying on phenomenological models rather than direct atomic-scale evidence. These gaps underscore the necessity of this critical review, which aims to unify the understanding of heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs) as a universal design principle, moving beyond case-by-case empirical optimization toward a predictive framework for high-performance AFE ceramics.
